title: On the Topological Invariance of Helicity
speaker: Oliver Edtmair
abstract: Helicity is an invariant of divergence free vector fields on a three-manifold. One of its fundamental properties is invariance under volume preserving diffeomorphisms. Arnold, having derived an ergodic interpretation of helicity as an asymptotic Hopf invariant, asked whether helicity remains invariant under volume preserving homeomorphisms, and more generally, whether it admits an extension to topological volume preserving flows. In this talk, I will present an affirmative answer to both questions for non-singular flows. The proof draws on recent advances in C^0 symplectic geometry, in particular regarding the algebraic structure of the group of area preserving homeomorphisms, which I will also survey. This is based on joint work with Sobhan Seyfaddini.

proof_stages.label: smooth helicity and Arnold's topological questions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines helicity of an exact divergence-free vector field on a closed three-manifold, recalls its smooth volume-preserving invariance and Arnold's asymptotic-linking interpretation, then formulates the homeomorphic conjugacy and C0-extension questions and the basic obstacles to attacking them directly.

proof_stages.label: nonvanishing main theorem and scope
proof_stages.summary: The main result is stated: helicity is invariant under volume-preserving homeomorphic conjugacy for smooth nowhere-zero vector fields, and it admits an extension to rest-point-free C0 volume-preserving flows with no positive-measure periodic orbit, with prior special cases and low-regularity variants placed in context.

proof_stages.label: measured-foliation/Hamiltonian-structure reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Nowhere-zero smooth vector fields and rest-point-free C0 flows are recast as one-dimensional measured foliations, called smooth or C0 Hamiltonian structures, so that helicity becomes an invariant of the structure rather than of a chosen volume form and vector field.

proof_stages.label: block insertion and the helicity-Calabi formula
proof_stages.summary: A local operation inserting a Hamiltonian isotopy into a trivial foliation chart is introduced, and in the smooth case the change in helicity is identified with the Calabi invariant of the inserted time-one map, depending only on its class in the abelianization.

proof_stages.label: C0 Calabi target group
proof_stages.summary: Using Banyaga's smooth Calabi theory and its C0 analogue for Hamiltonian homeomorphism groups of open surfaces, the speaker defines the universal abelian target group curly R and the extended Calabi projection to it.

proof_stages.label: universal extended helicity and its construction
proof_stages.summary: A unique curly-R-valued extension of helicity is characterized by smooth compatibility, homeomorphism invariance, and the C0 plug-insertion formula, and its existence is sketched by smoothing a C0 Hamiltonian structure outside flow boxes and localizing the remaining obstruction inside a C0 plug.

proof_stages.label: spectral-invariant input for injectivity
proof_stages.summary: External C0 symplectic results, especially linked spectral invariants and their commutative diagram with the smooth Calabi homomorphism, show that the natural map from real helicities into curly R is nontrivial and injective and that curly R admits projections back to the real numbers.

proof_stages.label: deduction of invariance and real-valued extensions
proof_stages.summary: The universal extension and injectivity imply equality of ordinary smooth helicities for homeomorphically conjugate structures, while choices of projections from curly R to the reals yield noncanonical real-valued C0 extensions.

examples.name: ideal incompressible fluid
examples.note: The Euler-flow vorticity field is used to illustrate why helicity is a conserved quantity under volume-preserving transport, not to supply the proof mechanism.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the helicity invariance argument to an incompressible ideal fluid by tracing vorticity as a pushforward under the fluid flow and concluding helicity is conserved

passes.reason: Used as the standard Euler-fluid motivation for helicity conservation, not as a structurally special or adversarial case.

examples.name: single periodic orbit of positive measure
examples.note: A pathological C0 flow whose one periodic orbit occupies most of the volume illustrates why the theorem excludes positive-measure individual periodic orbits and, later, why atoms in the transverse measure must be avoided.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes such a pathological periodic orbit by explaining that positive measure on one orbit would produce an atom in the transverse measure

passes.reason: A positive-measure single periodic orbit is a pathological atomic/collapsed transverse-measure case excluded as an obstruction to the theory.

examples.name: misaligned leaves across a flow box
examples.note: The red/orange leaf picture shows concretely why a homeomorphism smoothed on the complement of flow boxes may fail to extend, leading directly to the idea of inserting a C0 plug to reroute leaves.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the flow-box picture, showing how red and orange leaves fail to align and how a plug reroutes them to allow extension

examples.name: finite or nondegenerate isolated zeros
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that generic isolated zeros of a smooth vector field are a tractable kind of singularity beyond the no-zero hypothesis, unlike arbitrary wild zero sets.
examples.engagement_reason: finite or nondegenerate isolated zeros are only discussed in the Q&A as cases one might handle by approximation, without carrying out the procedure
